speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Laura Jean Brackett Albright took place at her home below Juliaetta on April 29th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: But it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The fact that, that the homesteading that you and your husband did was so late. How do you explain that that that that there was still this land available.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And nobody wanted it? It's rough land. Sam. It was real rough land. And when Raleigh came invalided home from Moscow and it was about 19. Let's see. 16. It was about two years before we were married, in 18. And there was a man who had filed on it and then threw it up because he didn't want it. And this is true because the place where the Johnson is, where that white House is, the old Albright house, they too, bought an exemption from a person that had filed earlier.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And but this person, I think he hoped that there would be coal. There was a big rush. And the old mine hole, they thought there was coal in the area because, when you go from here to Lawson, you see that black stuff that came down. Well, they. And there is coal. They burned it in a couple of times, but it was such small veins it didn't.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And then. So then they threw it up when it didn't do anything and Raleigh had had filed it, bought the exemption for $25. And then he took it over because it had joined his parents pasture. And then after we lived on it and proved up on it, 160 acres, then we took another option on this government land over here and took it a few more.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And that's why it was late, because it's it's rough land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say rough.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Hilly, stony. Just like this back here. Look out there, Sam. See how it is. And the only thing you ever could do on it would be to pasture it out. Some of the people thought at one time that they could plant trees, and others pine trees would grow apple trees. Seedling apple trees have grown up there.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And you know how it is. The people that don't have land and have a chance to get some free land and and some of the fellows thought that they could prove up on the and some of them did, and then got a job in Lewiston and would work or in Moscow and would come and work. And then another thing we had the rural schools, and it was easier for them to send their kids to rural schools than it was to move to Lewiston and send it to school and this sort of thing.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so, all they had to do was live there if they could make a living anywhere and, and they could have cows and chickens and pigs and, and a garden if they could find a spot. Little enough. Level enough. Our homestead shack was across in a gulch and it was 14 by 16. And Raleigh went up there with a pick and a shovel and an old horse and dug out a place, and we put the corner of that 12 by 16.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: This corner was about six feet in the ground, and that corner was eight feet off the ground on a on a post. And we lived there and, and proved up on it and lived there two and a half years. You had to. You had to move on to it in the first six months. You see, it took three years to prove up on it.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And we carried in our water because the only water that was available was in the creek and that wasn't fit to drink. So we carried it and bottles from over the homestead and carried in our food. And it wasn't too bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far of a distance did you have to hike then with the water from? Oh, was it from Raleigh's parents place? Yeah, she carried it.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Oh, what? A quarter of a mile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ... pretty much.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, no, along the hillside. Oh, yeah, a little bit. But then in the river, you start from the river and then just gradually angled off around it. It wasn't wasn't too difficult.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well 12 by 16.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's about the size of my of the lookout that I, that I.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yes. Well, we had, a little tiny stove, a little a little homesteader stove with four holes and a little tiny oven. And I'm a tall girl. So Raleigh put it up on a stand. He built a wooden stand, and we put the four little legs up there so that I could cook without bending over. And that corner of the cupboard was my was my kitchen.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And I had the spices and the flour and the stuff in a little thing about this big. And the dishes were next to it, and then the table was right next to that, and that was right by the foot of the bed. Then we had a big shelf that Raleigh put up with a curtain around it, and that was our clothes closet.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And on top of it was our, oh, we put hats and we put special books. And then on the other corner that we had this big chair, by the way. Come on, I'll have.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Raleigh was in Moscow and he went to a sale. Where did you say it was selling off? Up there. And for $15, he bought this old chair. And it's solid oak. This is the original seat. We've had it butchered 2 or 3 times, and I see it's got a split in here. But then after 58 years, what do you expect?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And this is the only thing that I brought. And this is the thing we took with us. And I brought all my children in that we didn't have, Raleigh had we had a cradle, of course. And over the bed when the children came, we strung a closed basket, you know, because Papa, mama could sleep down here and the baby was in the clothes basket.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: You have to be very inventive, Sam, when you live.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And Raleigh had had put a nice floor in it. Sometimes it was real rough. Some of the shacks that I've seen, they were foot boards with cracks in them. But Raleigh was a perfectionist and his father was a carpenter. And so because the bride was a schoolteacher, they put a they put a good floor in it, which was I was very thankful for.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And then we had a construction. You may not know what construction paper is. It's a.

They would put little paneling up and put this paper under there. Well, that was the only paper we had. And we never did get the inside. And then just tarp paper on the outside with the last side and one inch boards.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: But it was warm because of the paper. I mean, we just had.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: That could get along very well. After the other children came. Why we every time we'd have new baby, we'd put a new shed on one side of the other. But we we moved down. That wasn't the original shack because we rented Indian land that was next to it, where we could get a little bit closer because we had to get our children out to school.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so that was the one that we put the additions on.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: No, we never enlarged that.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: No, the baby was not born there because my mother in law was bound, determined that I should have a doctorate. Attendance, naturally. So the doctor wouldn't go over there. So we went over to her house, and the baby was born there, and the three children were born there. And then the last baby was born in the the homestead that we had on the Indian place.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And that was that's just right up here on the, on the.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many years did you actually live in your own homestead cabin on the on your homestead?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: We actually lived there about two years, really to stay there. We had because Raleigh, we had to make a living and there was a crop failure that third year. So he went back to the mechanical work and it went to Troy. And we spent the summer there in Troy, because there was about $160 dollars an acre that we had to pay, you know, for the I can't remember just what that was.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: It was in lieu of fences, because the fences were on top of that hill up there, and there's no road up there. And we didn't have any horse at the time, and we had to use our father's father in law's horses, my father in law's horses. And how do you get a horse up there to take posts and wire and so forth.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: So there was only three acres up there. So we did kill it, but we didn't fence it. And in lieu of that, I then we could pay an extra. It was an extra a month there. And Raleigh went to Troy and he was one of the original good auto mechanics. And so he earned enough money to pay our pay that and buy our food for the winter.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so then the next year, we rented the Indian land because Raleigh had been in the market gardening business, and this was a good thing for him. And he was outdoors where he needed to be. And so then that's when we moved to the shack down on the riverbank over here on the Indian land. It was we still used the homestead for, pasture, which was intended in the first place, and we were lucky enough to pick up some old range cows that they really were fattening for.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Butcher and Raleigh found out that they were bred. And so he came home and he said, you know, if we could get those cows, if we could afford to get the cows, we would have the calves. And then if one of them turned out to be a good milk or we'd have a milk cow for the youngsters. And so I took the last $112 of the money, I'd say, from teaching school, and we bought those cows like they all had calves.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And this is the way you get along. So, well, that's that that's that's bad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you're, husbands in inclination to, to farm was, was farming what he really wanted to do more than more than mechanics or. How did he look?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: No, he would rather have been a mechanic, but, he had a disability that he couldn't in the old days, you know, mechanics, instead of having hoists that they could put the cars up, they had to lay on a roller thing, you know, on the concrete floor. When he was a kid. That was before I knew him. The railroad people had their own fences, and there was a pipe gate like this with barbed wire through it, and kid, like he was going after the cows, and he decided he wouldn't bother to open the gate and jumped over it, and he fell and dislocated, fell and hit his side and dislocated.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Those vertebra bothered him all his life, and he could no longer lay on the concrete and do the work that he was supposed to do. And the doctor told him he had this good friend in Moscow that was a doctor. He used to drive for him because he needed to be out in the country, and Raleigh was a good driver.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Doctor.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Okay, well, Raleigh was the person that drove him, taught him to drive. And when he didn't drive, he drove him. And he kept the car in excellent condition. And he liked it. And it was Doctor Griffin that finally says, kid, you can't do this. He says, you got to get out. You got to get away from you. This you can't do this because your kidneys are in such shape that I won't guarantee you a life more than five years.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, it scared Raleigh, naturally. And so his folks then said, well, why don't you come on home? You'll be out and you can help us on the with the market gardening. And so when I came down, he was working with his own people at a market gardening. And, and then when it turned out that we would stay, that I would stay and that we would be married, we had to make some provisions.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, Raleigh already had this thing so poor, and all of them got together, and they built that little cabin over there because we were supposed to live on it. Raleigh, before he was married, wouldn't have to because his home was with his people. But after you get married, you changes your status. And so then we we thought of putting up a tent, but I wasn't too happy with the tent.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: My mother in law says, oh, no. She says, my goodness, you're not going to live in a tent. So she was the one that put the finger on the boys to get the lumber over there. And this is what happened.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, so the the cabin was built by the family. Really? Yes, yes.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Oh yes. Yes. I didn't do anything with it at all. I went home that summer, and then that spring I had a seven month school there, and I was home a month. And then the 7th of May we were married and I came back and the cabin was all built, but no one lived in it but us. After we left, I, we left it.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: We left some stuff stored there. And the first cyclone that I ever saw in this country blew it down, scattered it all up and down. My sterling silver where was scattered all up and down the hillside. And we had to retrieve it.

We seemed to get it in the spring, two Easter Sundays. And it wrecked my grandson's building over here because he felt real badly about it, because he says, well, the only things that were erected are the things I built.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, he built an open shed and he thought he had the studs buried deep enough, but he didn't. And the wind just naturally pulled him up and blew it over behind the old building and smashed it away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, let me ask you, how you decided that you were interested in becoming a teacher.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: I was the oldest of a large family. And as the oldest girl with an ill mother, I had been taking charge of the family more or less. And I graduated from high school at Gifford in the first class of the Gifford High School way back in 1916, and.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: I had been teaching filling in for a long time, and I found that it was just real fun to teach the lower grades while I was this high school kid, you know? And there's just something of the gleam in the youngsters eye when he catches that gleam of what you're trying to teach him. And I needed to make some money to get a job and make some money.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so because my mother was a teacher, she encouraged me. So I went to Moscow and took the examination and taught that that's how I got my. And I loved it. I loved the teacher, I taught for terms before I was married, and I when I say four terms, I talked two full terms and then filled in for other teachers that that for some reason rather didn't feel there.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: In fact, one year I talked my own seven months term in Jacks Canyon, up your little girls school. I went home to look out and that teacher had was ill and couldn't finish. And according to the law they had to have seven months and there was still three months there that that wasn't finished. And then that was the reason that I came down here, because that teacher ran away and got married and she didn't finish hers.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so I had to teach six weeks there to finish out seven months. Her. And so that's the way I got to. And then that fall, I wasn't going to teach at all because my mother was still bedridden and was ill, and because teachers were were scarce. You see, we were teaching for 60 and $65 a month, and mostly you could most of the young people were getting more than that at the time, and I had planned to stay home and take care of my mother.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Then we had a new county superintendent of Mrs. Foster, and I think if Mrs. Redfield had stayed, she knew the situation. It would not have put the pressure on me. But Mrs. Foster sent me kind of a nasty letter saying, well, you the state has granted you a certificate, and if you're not going to use it, maybe we should give it to someone else.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: It wasn't quite that bald, but it frightened me because I had spent my savings in the summer school and had raised my certificate from a third grade to a second grade. And so I showed it to my father and he says, well, honey, he says, I guess you better go. So I took the next train in the middle of October and came down here.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And I've been here ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This job was waiting for you.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Then I guess so, I guess so I had all grades. It's a good thing I'd had before. I had 18 pupils, all grades and ages from five and a half to 18. You see, it's hard. That's the rural school that you get into. And it takes some doing to to keep that many pupils interested in and occupied and, and so on.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: But people, pupils in a rural school as a rule will. Oh look after little kids. You know they're coming in. These youngsters that were so young were walking two and a half miles in snow and sleet and no busses in those days, you know, and so two of the girls schools, children would see that they got home because I was worried too, I didn't you can't turn a five and six year old kid out in the storm to walk through four miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the school here?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Pilot Rock. It's now when you. When you came down from Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I came from from through Juliaetta.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah. When you come from Juliaetta, you didn't realize what you. When you were going across the bridge to that little creek. That's a and then that big rocky thing comes down, you go round. And you saw a home there. Well, that's my old schoolhouse. That's the old pile of rocks to house.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: When we consolidated in 1949, 1951, I worked on the county committee. I'd been 41 years in school board work after I quit teaching, and they put me on the school board. And so I worked there. And so I was named a member of the consolidation committee for County, and I was there to consolidate that rural school, you see, with the district.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And it was interesting to me because in the old days I had tried to interest them in in consolidating it with Juliaetta, which seemed fine. They already had busses coming down off the ridges. And I thought, well, we're close enough. Why? Why not take those pupils up there? And then I worked on the board to consolidate and send them clear over to life.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Because Juliaetta was consolidated with Kendrick, you see?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your background, what you had to learn to teach you took summer school courses. Is that the way you got prepared? And you did this at the university at Moscow? Yes. Did this take very long to to be.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Six weeks? No, 6 to 9 weeks. It's a summer course. I would go in about in June and stay till last July and middle of August and then. And you well, you had to work, but it wasn't any harder really than, than

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: I don't know what else you'd say. It wasn't any harder than for me to prepare and have the lessons ready for eight grades of school teacher. And we were supposed to have a lesson plan so that when our district superintendent would come to visit, it was posted on the on the wall. I usually wrote mine on the board because I had to change it.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And you had to cool, coordinate and correlate. There's seven subjects, and from the fifth grade through the eighth grade, they all have their own degree of geography reading, spelling, physiology. You went through that and sure. Didn't you have all those things when you went to school? I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I had pretty much.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah. And so I would correlate now, for instance, in physiology, I would a physiology book that is right for a seventh and eighth grader. They have to read that. And so I would have that in the reading lesson. And then I could and then spelling, sometimes I would put the fifth, fourth and fifth grades together and they would have the spelling.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And then I would appoint some of the youngsters that would like to, to hear the spelling. And it was interesting because they had to correct it, and if they didn't correct it, well then they got a mark down. I mean, they didn't notice that it was spelled wrong or what have you. And we had spelling books in those days.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: It wasn't all oral spelling. And so it was easy to delegate some of this. And then we had to teach penmanship. Well, I had to work that in too, because some of those you'd be surprised some of the sixth graders would be better. Penman at all. Syrian where you did it, you know. And so the youngsters and we'd, we'd make a I'd make a test of it.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Not real school because you can't expect the fourth and fifth graders, which are getting to that age where they're pretty independent, to look to a seventh and eighth graders with authority unless they want to. So we would have spelling bees, and we would have writing bees, and we would have reading afternoon reading hours. And I'd invite the mothers in and I did all kinds of things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had the parents come, parents would come in and take part in this.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, listen, they some of them did. They were interested and some of them couldn't be bothered. You know how it is. But the teacher was always expected to have a literary to every week. Oh, boy. And so our memory things that I'd say. Well, now look, Elmer, for instance, he was a slow learner. I said, Elmer, if you would learn this verse of of six lines, you can be in the Literary Friday.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, sometimes it worked. And then the singing. We had an old organ there, thank goodness. I'd had a little family music lessons. And so we, we did our singing, we learned, and 2 or 3 of the youngsters would be a little better. And so we had some solos and we had some duets. And the school teachers in those days were looked to for I wonder how to say this for the intellectual leadership of a community.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And this literary business was part of it. And the part that I didn't like was every Friday night they had a debate. And this was for the not for the schoolchildren, but for the community. And my trouble with the debate was to keep it from being too personal, acrimonious, and to keep the themes.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Decent.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: You get into a backwoods and there's a certain element that they have a barnyard humor, sense of humor, and oh, it's amazing. And you can't or you shouldn't alienate those people. And how do you do it nicely?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what would the kinds of subjects be that, that they might get carried away with?

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, there was a neighbor up the last ravine that had a range bull, and he was he was never they never could keep him where. And he would get out and breathe. And he called. And within five miles that was in heat. And so some of the people's better range cows and some of their milk cows would be bred by this rangy bull that was neither milked or what have you.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so that spoiled the cows years of work because the calf, all you could do with that calf would be to kill him. So Levi came up one day and he says, I have a good one. He says one that that is that we need to sow on the board, he wrote. He says, resolved that Levi's bull.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Resolved that every cow that Levi's bull and pregnant can charge the owner of the cow for stud services. Wow. So then.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: So then on the other side, the fellow, the next one came back. And this was a woman, and she she still lives up there. She says, to hell with that. She said, I will put a better one up there. So she went up and wrote, she says, everybody that has a cow that is impregnated by that bull can sue him for damages.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so, well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There you are in.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: The middle. So there. And I was it they thought that it was going to throw me, I think. But after all. And then another silly one was in those days, they didn't wear a long hair. And one of the young men there had gone to up in the woods to work. And when he came home, he had the prettiest curly hair you ever saw.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: He was a gross clothes. And they had there was a Negro strain in that family way back. And you know how that beautiful, that hair, that Negro hair that comes down. And so he came literary and he didn't cut it. So one of the things that we debated that night was whether we should cut grocery, not I mean, just just entertainment, just crazy stuff.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Well, then on the serious side, the, that was about the time that they talked consolidation and we really had several sessions of whether or not the schools should be consolidated and that this was a serious one. But from the ridiculous to the serious, I gave you that.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Yeah, well, I was born in Minnesota, too, and near Saint Paul. Just my father came out here working for the warehouse of people as an estimator in opening new timber lands in Oregon, British Columbia. I, I've lived here, up there when I was six years old and and then he finally lost his health in that terrible Minnesota fire that lasted six months.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: So he came back and took over because he already owned the Lookout Lumber Company, a beer lookout. That's how I got back here. And then, of course, we lived here since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How how did how did that happen to him in the in that losing his health in the fire.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: The terrible forest fires in the Wisconsin, Minnesota raged.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Of getting the logs to the to the Atwood Lumber Company, which was in Park Falls, Wisconsin at the time. It was his job. He had charge of all the camps, the river, the logging and the railroad that got. And so when that fire raged so long there, they fought it. But they and dried timber, you just it was about six weeks that my mother didn't know whether my father was alive or not.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And it was a terrible forest fire. And and when he came home, he had breathed so much of the smoke because he had a railroad car. It was supposed to be on the railroad. But you can't send send men into a dangerous situation. He led these men, and that's why he had been working overtime, you see, and had an extra dose.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: So when he finally came home exhausted and sick, why, he ended up in the hospital with him since his on his sons. And in those days there was no they did pay the doctor bill and part of the hospital bill, but there was no no sick leave. There was no sick payment. speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And so it it was a civilizing the first time that I took charge of a program. I was seven years old and we had a Christmas program and a mother, of course, helped me. And so one of the things that I'll always remember and that helped me in public relations, all these men, there was about 150 of them were in the cook shack.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: To see where they ate was the biggest thing that we had. There wasn't any home or any officer or any kitchen, but they all had to eat, you see. So this is where we were. And she said, so we had some songs and we led some songs, while some of them could sing and some couldn't.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And then I when I got ready, when we got ready to have the Christmas tree, and my mother had helped me fix some homemade candy things for all of these fellows, and there was all of these Christmas goodies with popcorn, and I don't know where they got all this stuff. So then we were going to spend the hour telling about the best Christmas we ever had, and it was real popular.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: Some of the fellows could be funny, but that's the first time I ever saw grown up men really wiped their eyes because here they were, way out there, away from their families, and when I told them about the best ones, they'd start thinking back because they were then the first thing you know, the tears would be going down and I didn't know what to do.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And mother realized that I was kind of at it. And so she came over to my side and she put her arm around me and she said, and we are here now. If there's any special requests that you'd like to have, we'll do the best we can. So we wound up that way, and mother played and sang, and I sang to things that I couldn't even remember.

speaker: Lora Brackett Albright
words: And that fellow would come up and and sing and or did he like jingle bells up here for the sake of the rest of them? words: And so we had a wonderful two hours. And you see participation. Participation. We were leaving there because dad was changed, sent other places. In fact, we were sent back to Minnesota.
